speaker: Sam Schrager

words: For $23, Emmett, what year was that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Long about the same time. Now that was terrible. And that paid $5 to get Hall's full gas. That's about $15.

words: What was the, when did you two start going together? That was I? Yeah, when was that?

words: Oh, 25. I think I took it out once in 25 and I think I took it out two times in 26.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Three times in 27?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Something like that. You got married in 29. No, I don't know. Well, um... She was going to school down at Lewiston.

words: And oh, it's a big tale.

words: Well, anyway, um, what did you start to tell?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, I was going to finish that. Now let's...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, I was going to say he walked up to me on the street and he said, uh, what did you say to me now?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, you was with John your brother. Yeah. And he was in the Ford going downtown and John stopped him or hollered at me. I just called you good looking. Oh, that's it. That's all. High good looking or something like that. All the girls got the same moniker. He sure thought he was cute.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So did all the girls. But you know. Really, we started going together in 25. Well, see, I went to school in Lewiston in 25 and 26 and I finished down there in the fall of 26. I went to summer school in the fall of 26 and then I went up to the school in the fall of 26. And then I went up here at the Woodfell School above Harwood, you know, way up there at Parker's. And I taught school up there and that's when we went, started going together.

words: I just turned it on. Just a minute ago. Oh, Lord. But I wanted, well, don't, I wanted to know when, how you guys got together. In the old days, it seems like those aren't such old days, but it really seems a lot different than the nowadays, you know, as far as young folks meeting and deciding they're going to make a life together.

speaker: Sam Schrager

words: Well, they have trouble with the day. They make that judgment too fast, I think. Three or four time years to size one another up for you like a horse. Like a couple of bulls out there trickling around a while before they go out. Well. When you were going, you were going to school there in Lewiston, Lewiston Normal.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. Yes. And she taught. And then I taught up in Woodville two years and then Chambers Clack. And in the meantime, I substituted one year over here, one quarter. And I guess that takes us up to 29. She would teach at school. Oh, I don't know what year it was. Then I started again in 43, you know, during the war. That's during the war. They had that writing on it. Yeah, that was writing. And then I talked to 49.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when, was courting a very formal kind of thing? I mean, did you get engaged very early? Did you just date more or less like kids do nowadays?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: It was Christmas in 48 and we were married in September 49. 28. 29. 29. 29.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got engaged in Christmas at 28?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: 14 and 28. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Why did I say 40?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You were thinking of teaching. That's what I was talking about. Yeah. War business. I know you remember probably much about that gasoline rationing they had, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The second war?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. No, that was before I was born. I figured. Anyway, you know, I was sawing down the mill. Had saw you. They had four carriages, you know. Each one had a head saw you. You know. Yeah. Anyway, and she was teaching school up here at Chambers Flat. And I had about eight or nine head of cows, a bunch of beef cattle to feed and take care of. And I was running a farm too. And so I had to have, you know, three gallons of gas a week, you know, as your allotment. Three gallon a week was running too. Well, I had that much on two cars. So I had that old Dodge, old 27 Dodge. We had that. And then I had this 42 Plymouth. And so she had to go to school and I had to go to the mill. We had to take a cream to town and run around. I went over there to the office in Moscow to try to get some extra gas. No. No, just couldn't do it. Seems like everybody went there to get extra gas. They figured it was a damn liar, you know. He didn't need it, you know. So I went over a couple times and they turned me down. No, I just can't give you any extra gas. I told them why I needed it for them. So, my gosh, she had to go to school. I had to go to work. I had to do my running around on the farm. You know, just take cream to town. And we were just going day and night, both of us. And so I just didn't change my speed around the cars. I could get gas to farm with, barrel gas, but you wasn't supposed to use that on the road, you know. I could get all the gas I wanted for the barrel, but then I couldn't get the tickets to go car gas. So I just pumped gas into the car tax and kept on going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that marked gas, the farm gas? Was the farm gas marked?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, you had to rise them. But they'd go be your speedometer every month. You had to turn in your speedometer reading, you know. Well, I just turned in. In order to get that three gallon a week, I had to turn it in. So I went on for a month, I guess, in France. I got a letter from the head office. They wanted to see me over there. So I knew what it was about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, how come, well, what's the beef?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: It seems like you said your car is making pretty good gas mileage. I said, no, no, I don't think it is. I said, I got two of mine. I don't think either one of them is making any extra ordinary gas mileage. Well, according to the speedometer reading, there's an amount of gas you're supposed to have. I said, no. I said, no, I'm burning gas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where are you getting the gas?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I said, getting it out of the farm barrel. Oh, boy, he's right through me in jail right now. That old, that old, that guy that used to run them cedars over there at Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kenworth?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Kenworth. Yeah, he was the head of it. So I told him, well, he wanted to know what I was using the gas for. I said, well, my wife is driving about eight or ten miles a day, going to school and back. I'm holding the job down in the mill. I'm rucking so many cows. And I said, I'm doing some farm work, farming. And then I says, because you can do that on three gallon of gas a week. And you come over and show me how.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, he says, you said your wife teaches in school?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I said, yeah. I said, I'm holding the job down in the mill. I'm a farmer. I'm rucking so many cows, chickens, pigs to feed.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And, well, he says, why didn't you say something about that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I said, Mr. I've been over here and cried my eyes out trying to get some extra gas, legal. So, well, he said, you go home. You'll get some extra gas. So the next day, I got home the next day in the mail. There was a string of tickets at Chogan -Ox. Well, I couldn't start to use them all, you know. It's crazy. So, by gosh, you know, but if you take them extra tickets and turn them back, you didn't need them. Well, then they'd charge you that much. So I just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd do what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They'd charge you that much next time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd credit you?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, well, you just turned in and say you burnt 60 gallons that month. Well, then if you take a bunch of them tickets and turn them back, that's all you'd get is just 60 gallons. Well, then if you had an emergency, you know, you wouldn't have enough gas. So I'd never turn the tickets back. And I didn't, you know, waste gas or do anything loose because I didn't have time to go out and joy ride.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they know that you were turning the speedometer back, were you?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you turning the speedometer back or you couldn't?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, I let the speedometer back. That's why it got me in trouble. Oh, I see, I see. If I had to turn the speedometer, if I had to turn the speedometer, then I could have got by. But I just let it go because... Well, I'm mad at them. Let them throw me in jail. So they want to throw a hard -working old farmer in jail, let them throw it to it. But anyway, I just let the speedometer run. Just didn't put the message at all. So that's why I got it if you call me up because I was getting too good at my gas mileage. Them cars. Yeah, right. So anyway... I guess bureaucracy isn't a new problem. No. They had the same problem back then. Yeah, that's what they had then.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So anyway, after that, how did they go from one extreme to the other?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You see, I told them about how much gas I needed, but anyway, on the next day, here comes a string of tickets. Man, oh man. Well, her dad worked on the section. He was the section boss on the railroad. And so during the winter, he just laid the crew off, you know, and he just kind of worked himself on the track. He had to shovel out the crossings one thing or another. So he just put his car up. So by gosh, he was getting extra gas for driving to work and doing his work on the railroad. But then when the next one come, he hadn't been running his car. In fact, he lived up there by Harvard and he left the set. So he's a good -ass guy. He takes his tickets he didn't use and take them and turn them in.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So the guys from the next spring, when he wanted to get his car, they couldn't go away. He was free of the gas. That's all he could get. They wouldn't give him any more. So I helped him out with tickets. It wasn't fair, but it wasn't fair to him either.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So that's the way democracy works. You're a liar until you prove yourself honest.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You ought to take some of them cookies home, Lord. Let her try them. Maybe she'll buy you some. Yeah, I could take a handful. I'll get her. I'll put some in the sack. I want her to try them out. Say goodbye for you. You started telling me how you two got together. You said it was a long story. Oh boy. I don't know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Shall I tell that story how it took us a long together?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It tells how the way people used to live.

words: You ain't going to burn my book, are you?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no. But he's got that turned on. Oh come on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it a bad story?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, it was a... Well, yeah, I'll go ahead. Anyway, we started going together in 25. And she had to go back to school. Well, anyway, she stayed with a girlfriend. I won't name her. She's gone now. But anyway, they got to... That girl said she could bet she could cut out of time with me. So, it was foolish enough to take her on. So she tried it. Well, I didn't know what the devil the rip was, you know. Her brother -in -law and I guys wanted me to go to the house. The other girls. The other girls. Brother -in -law. And I didn't know what the heck the rip was. So I stayed pretty skunch out of that mess. I didn't know what the heck it was. I might have changed my mind. It might have been more fun to think. But anyway, I'd write to her and she wouldn't answer me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anna wouldn't?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, she wouldn't write back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Because she made a bargain with that girl. She'd lay off of me while she could get me. You know, it's something to have two girls fighting over you, but... No, we made a bet, see.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And it was to last, the bet was to last nine weeks, wasn't it, Emmett?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't know. You bet it. I didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anna, why'd you do something like that? Why'd you take a chance like that? You were that sure of this guy?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, you're never sure of him. Anyway, I didn't know what was going on. So finally she wouldn't answer my letters. So I thought, the heck of women. And they went and bought me a new Harley -David's motorcycle. Well, that just about broke me in business, you know, by that brand new. And so I just went and tied it up down the road here with that motorcycle and had more fun than Pack, Heck, and Mile. And so...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Having more fun than what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Pack, Heck, and Mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear that expression?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You never heard that? Never heard that expression?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Pack, Heck, and Mile. Pack, Heck, and Mile. I don't know. I'll try to start with. Try to stay up. Anyway, so I'd give up on her. I thought, heck. Then it comes February. I had a love affair with that motorcycle. Until about February she came up here. He had broken his ankle. Bone in his ankle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the motorcycle?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. I was down in the car. I backed up. I was going down that hill in the potlatch.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You know, down halfway down the hill that concrete wall lives there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I was at night. I was going down there. Car came up and turned up in there. Well, he went out of sight. So I was batting along down that hill there. See, the old bikes just had a back wheel brake. And so anyway, I was like, I just went up there, and then pretty quick, here he come back out. Backing out into the highway. Well, I didn't know whether you were going to keep a go on or what, but... Anyway, I zigged and zagged him. He backed out into the highway, and I kept turning and going behind him. I thought he'd stop, see. Well, he didn't. I guess he figured if he'd get far enough back, I'd cut in front of him. Well, we both zigged him. Well, we should have been zagged. But he had no business backing into the highway that way. Anyway, I got the motorcycle cramping down sideways, and I slid into him sideways. Oh, I broke a couple bones in my foot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were laid up?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, I laid up for a few days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Anna came up to see you?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, I was off the mill, so I didn't have nothing to do. So I had a dance up here in Princeton, so I come up to the dance, watch the rest of them have fun, and she was there to dance. Well, when the thing hold changes, it changed that. We started to go together again then. That's when I came home from Myrtle. Yeah, that's where she... Myrtle is where she went teacher training.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: What's...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The women were that fickle when you were a kid, young?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I don't think most of them were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As fickle as this?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But I could be talked into most anything then. That girl was so sure, and I was just as sure that... Uh -uh. You know, and I wasn't... You know, I wasn't that much for looks. I don't know why I was so conceited. Oh, you weren't too bad. You're so good looking now that I imagine you must have been really a beauty when you... She wasn't a bad looking pill. I can't believe that. She was a pretty good looking bitch. I'll bet. No, I don't know what... You're not that girl, you know. She was so bold. And never had a relationship working for her, you know. This is Emmett. You see, he hasn't changed an awful lot. This is our wedding picture. Oh, you're good looking in that picture. And, see, he hasn't changed all that much. Well, you know, you're really a lovely looking girl. You're both a good looking. You're a good looking couple. I hate to say it, Emmett, but you're pretty good looking too there. But on the picture, now he doesn't... His wife doesn't keep his hair as nice as he did here. Anyway... But you really took a chance like that, that she'd be able to... Yes, exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you sure?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Did you really think that he wouldn't fall for it, or did you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You weren't so sure that you liked him, or what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She wasn't too much look after it. No, I... She... Well, she was a person that always tried to work the other guy, you know. If she wanted to come up, go to a dance up here, you know, she'd nigger me all week to write Emmett a letter and ask him to come down and get us and take us to the dance, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What if he'd come down to take me to a dance?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Why, she'd go, you know. But that was too far and too much. But for three months... That's good. A good time, too. But if you had to let go for three months, you couldn't be sure what it would be like in three months' time. No. But... You probably weren't all that serious about him. Oh, we just started going together, really, you know. Well, you know, we did, because we couldn't find anybody else by that time. So... That's what you think.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Now I... Now I just went and bought another motorcycle and I had the love of it. Another guy down at the kid at my age, he got one just like it. And boy, we were just a yellow streak up and down the road, you know. And that's true. They were... They just... He took me for a ride one day.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Soon after he got his bike, didn't you, Emmett?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, that summer, next spring. Oh, well, anyway... I got on behind him, you know, and he was making a good, slow ride up there. I made an awful mistake there. And I bet he went...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: What was it, you went 90 miles an hour?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, we went from where she was living over in Princeton up to Harvard, turned around and come back. I think I was going home by 10 minutes, you know. And that was gravel roads, rock roads. Oh, I don't know where we went 90 miles, but I was bumping better than 80, I'm sure. Yeah, well, that's a real nice guy. And then I went up there and wheeled right back together. And that was the only time I ever rode on the bike. Only time she'd seen daylight bike. That's when she got on and when she got off, because she had her feet by my back, you know. Oh, we were going so fast and that wind was... But anyway, if we had taken it... Easy. Easy, you know, I might have learned, you know, how to like it as well as the girls do nowadays, you don't know. Oh, yeah, it's been a lot of fun. But as it was, you must have been pretty mad. No, I wasn't. Or pretty scared anyway. Well, I think I delight in scaring people. I don't know why I done it. You know, I thought I'd have something to kick before I done, but like one time I was going potlatching a tramp going down the road. I had a big pack on his back, just down in Bluehampton. So I stopped and I couldn't go on the ride. He looked at that motorcycle and said, no, he thinks he's just going to walk. I said, I'm going to potlatch, he's going to ride. I just talked him into getting on the motorcycle. I did motor it, you know. And so I got him on and he had that big pack on his back too. Hey, we went for potlatch. Well, I got down there where them curves going up the hill there, you know. Well, you know, in a motorcycle, you know, you lean to go around this way and you lean to go down that way. Well, I knew what he'd do when I hit that first curve and I leaned over. I knew he'd go this way, you know, he's going to be on top of it. So I allowed for it, he was waiting to pack and made it pine. And every time I hit a curve, I could lean this way or that way. Well, he'd go just the opposite way. And I stopped down there in front of the, what used to be the old pool hall there, potlatching. He got up and he was just a -shaking. And he thanked me so nice for that ride, you know. He surely had way to kill me, you know. He either cussed me out, you know, for what I'd done. I'd done the fun. But he was so quiet about it and thanked me so much for that, for giving me a ride down there, you know. It just kind of made me feel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you cuss him out?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, you wouldn't have cussed him out, but did you give him a hard time for that or were you just... No, I... Well, she didn't get her breath for two weeks. Sometimes she got away with it herself. I got about it. No, I didn't, I just wouldn't ride again. That was it. I've been involved for about six. But if he had a sidecar too, you know, I could have ridden in that, you know. No, I didn't have it then. I traded it all. I got it with the motorcycle. I just wanted them sidecars. If you had had it then. Well, that's fun riding them.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You never ride in a sidecar?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Never, never had one. Oh boy. They was easy riding. And then that's fun too. If you want to go around the curve, like you come around here and you want to go around this curve, you know, why you just turn it a little sharp and that sidecar goes up in the air. Even if you got a person in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh yeah, you put it right up in the air and as long as you hold it there, you know, you can hold it right up there. Well, I never was too good at that, but I know we went down to the races down in McMinnville, Oregon. I was down there out in McMinnville. And they had motorcycles and I had a cop out there. And he had a sidecar on. I don't know how many girls, there must have been at least six girls besides him on that motorcycle sidecar. And he'd go up and back and forth in front of the grad stand with them girls up there. He'd raise them up and let them down, raise them up and let them down. They was yelling, you know. I don't know how he'd done it with all that weight on it. Must have been fun riding in there. Your mother, you know, you took her up to Wallis. And she enjoyed it so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the sidecar, huh?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, yeah, she rode the sidecar. You know, when my mother told me about dating when she was young, you know, it sounded pretty crazy to me. I mean, I thought kids were pretty wild these days. But, you know, when she told me about it, it sounded just about as bad. It sounded like you could be engaged to three different fellas at once pretty easily, you know. Breaking one and starting another one. And a lot of, as if they didn't really, as if a lot of young people didn't really know what marriage and love was about in those days either. No. Is what the feeling I got. I had an old Model E Ford. And then the first year she taught school, she taught at a place they called Woodfell. I don't know, it's just this side of where you go into the temple when you're on the north and south highway. Yeah, I know about where that is. She had a law school after that. That's where she first started teaching school. And it's no getting deep up anywhere. There's no car running much, you know. Everybody's ready to use horses for slaves. Winter time. And when I got in and out, I'd go in every weekend, bring her out and take her back Sunday evening. And make tracks, you know, car tracks. Well, it's all in a kind of breeze. Well, it's just like a railroad track. You know, going through the snow, your car kind of shifts a little bit once in a while, but you couldn't steer it. Once you got started, the other side of the highway was in them ruts. I could go clear up on that old road. You couldn't hold the snow, you'd have to follow them ruts. Just like a railroad track. And I'd go in there and pick her up. And the guys would have been over the street with the snow on the road. But it was packed down, you know. And all winter long, I went in and out. If you ever jumped a track, well, you only had to get out and shovel and get it back up and get it lined up and then packed down the snow where you jumped and easeled across it. Once you get back on the track again, well, you went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the people put their cars up in those days? You were probably one of the few that kept driving in the winter?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, up there. See, the snow gets so deep up there. And they didn't use to have snow plows. And the people just put their cars up and go to sleighs, horses. And there's only one other guy that lived up there that was driving. That old man Boone. Yeah, and his home was in Palouse, so he'd... But they had a meal up there, and he'd come up to the meal, you know, once in a while. When the road was bad, he didn't go. That was almost my private... He saw him run the meal, and he'd just come up once in a while. That was the other car that was running up there. She had a place up there she was boarding up there, and my railroad track went right up to that gate. And I had a place where I could back up and start down again. If you wanted to go pulling that, you was out of luck. Because the snow got deep, and you had to keep your track broke out all winter. But the snow didn't drift up there much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did couples do for entertainment, you know, going together? What was the entertainment around here in those days?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Usually a show in Palouse or... You go to Palouse for shows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Movie?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And then once in a while at Harvard. No, not Harvard then. But there's dance here in Princeton we could go to if we... No, in the wintertime, I don't know, kids just entertain themselves. It was just weekends, is what I'd seen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Movie show in Palouse? It was movies then?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they had movies in Palouse. They had a theater down in Potlatch too.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah,

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: they had quite a deal.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: People would go to Shilwell on a Saturday night. On their way back there. Of course, you wouldn't know where the old road was now. It's still there, but it's not traveled much anymore.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But you know where Candy Fort is?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Highway comes around there now. Well, right there at Candy Fort, that hall. You'd come from Palouse, you'd turn right, left. And it goes around up over the hills and then down comes outdoors by that lumber company. Where that lumber company would blow pot lights there. That's where the road would come out. It would come down over them hills. Anyway, we'd come up there one night and it was muddy and raining. A whole bunch of us in the car had that old beating of a dance. And I had to cut out wide open. It was better like a bull. Anyway, we got over there, we just had to pass that Candy Fort outfitting up over the hill there. Well, the headlights were all up above somebody's head. It was just ruts up today, you know. Mud roads. And there was nothing but headlights. It was as far as you could see. And so there was a car stuck up there. Nobody could get by him. Oh, gosh, you know. I looked up on the bank fence along there, but there was a bank out there so far from the fence. I said, lookin' good. I head up on that bank and I bounced for ditches. I would clear up that whole bunch, you know, in the ruts and in the ditches and up on the banks. That whole car hurt like it. But I passed everything. And boy, oh boy, everybody in the country had a powerful wheelie. It wasn't a wheelie, it was just a damn bull driving the thing, you know. He was lucky. Yeah, he was lucky, oh boy. That's why I didn't upset the damn thing. Anyway, we had a lot of fun. Everybody in the car was just yelling, you know, hold on to her. And anyway, I bet there was 40, 50 cars in that string lined up there. And I would run the whole bunch and land on the head of them and kept on going. And boy, that old powerful wheelie, well that old powerful wheelie had a 40 -horse power motor in it. But it had an exhaust pipe about that big around it. It was better like an old tractor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents, did they object at all to your going with such a wild guy?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh boy. No, they weren't too much. But different Harvard people, see I was raised up at Harvard. Well, it was like Dogpatch.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: It was like a little, what do they call them guys back there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Dogpatch or... Lavner. No, it was, well, family was always fighting back there. McCallies and that. Oh yeah, yeah. Harvard and Princeton was quite like the country back there. You know, they... A bunch of those old women would get along on the side of the line and they'd look at you and know you was like you was walking out from Mars or something. Princeton, I didn't have much use for Harvard ice either. So, I went along that way for years. So when I started going at it... Oh boy, I don't know. And I used to, I could pay my ass for it. Oh yeah, I did. I'd do up there with that old billy, you know, and the bell would up trip and you'd go up there and you'd go up there and you'd go up there and you'd leave a dust creek, you know, by the time you leave Princeton, you'd get up there and that old car bellied. You'd come around the corner and there'd be an old farmer out there next to the fence, whoa boy, holding all these horses. Hey boy.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So you know about take -all. Yeah, I know about take -all. And then when you started going there, they probably thought it was too bad for her. Yeah, I suppose. My dad said, I asked him one time, he said, well, I suppose you'll have a better time, you know, have more fun if you go with a guy like him than somebody, you know, I don't even know who he compared him with now. Probably one of them high -related stuff there. Probably some farmer boy. But anyway, he, he was, always was good to him. When we had our combine down here, he'd come down and help him harvest and he was, they were real good pals. Yeah, he'd get some low -sacks for me.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, we got along all right. Well, did you do a lot of planning before you got married, thinking about, you know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Planning?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I mean getting married and what you would need to be married as far as, you know, setting up a house and all that stuff, thinking about the future.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did kids do that in those days?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, I just kind of went from day to day. You didn't have a job teaching at the wall yet, I don't think. Anyway. No, but I had one more year before I'd have to go back to school and we talked about the mill. And then the mill shut down. Well, they had the little water and they didn't have enough water to float the log down at the dam, the potlatch, you know, the logs were laying on the ground. So they had to shut the mill down.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So I told Anna, well, why don't we go get married and go on our honeymoon?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And all the mill shut down. So that's what we did. And then we come back and we went to Garfield and got our stove and... Rented the house down potlatch. Rented the house, well, after a while. We couldn't get one right off. It was a few weeks, wasn't it, before we got one. Yeah, I don't know. We had Marjorie in for a house and finally got it. And then we moved in with our bedroom set and our kitchen out there.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I had that one in the shoestream, they call it, they say. Got what we needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went to Garfield to get a stove?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, yeah, that's where we bought a stove. They had a hardware over there. Called the name of Ireland, ran us a hardware store. So we went over there and then we bought some other furniture down at Gibby Williams's. Pluice. And then that spring, we bought a... No, you had a .22 and I got me one. Yeah, we used to go squirrel shooting, so I got her a little .22. Just a little one. But man, we were deaf on squirrels for a while. And then I just couldn't see them at all. I just missed every shot. She was a Deadeye.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: What do they call her? What was her Deadeye want?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, that's not a... But anyway, I could shoot a wire. We were not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where a wire is taken to a post?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She could split the wire. No kidding. Not only once, she could do it two or three times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use any of that food? Did you use any of that for food when you shot the animals? Or did you use the skins or just for play?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, just to get rid of them. Farmers were almost paying bounty on them to get rid of them. Squirrels, sure. You don't see them much anymore now. They're pretty well done rid of them. Boy, when I was a kid around here, Jesus Christ. Right up this road, there'd be squirrels running back and forth across the road in front of your car all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get chivaried after you got married?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, we had a... They gave me a shower up in Harvard and I had one down here to Princeton. And then we had a chivarie and it was just beautiful. Yeah. I got so many, many... Well, two showers, you know. It worked out all right except one of your old boyfriends had the chivarie. And I just had a box of cigars out here. There wasn't that many people here. But somebody told me afterwards he went out with pockets full of cigars. Well, he got something out of it anyway. He got something out of it. Yeah, but that was... By the way, spell shivery. Oh, I thought there was two ways. The easy way I've seen it is S -H -I -V -A -R -E -E. But I've also seen it spelled C -H, like shaviary or something. Yeah, it's French. I think it's spelled the French way. C -H -A -R -I -V -A -R -I. Yeah. It doesn't sound like shivery, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's pronounced shivery too?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I guess so. You look at my dictionary, shivery, and that's where it's spelled and that's what it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the showers like then, Anna? Did they give you gifts?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, gifts. Pillow slips, you know, or blankets or a lot of dishes. I got that covered back in there. Most of them. A lot of wedding gifts in there. I'll have to divide up one day.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No. They don't shivery anymore. I haven't heard of shivery around here anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they surprise you? Was that part of it? I mean, did you know the one was coming or what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Just surprised you. Wait until you get to bed, you know. That makes you get up to dress.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I know one time I got up there and got shot one time with a shivery. My sister got mad. We was living on the other place up there. Sleeping up there, you know. So, I think guys, when they started to come in, they was shooting shotguns off and beating on cans, you know. And we had an old shepherd dog. And I never seen a dog as brave as he was, but he went out and met the whole bunch of them. They were banging away and raising their neck, you know. And he went out to any man teaching school. We were printing them. Alfred Chaba. By the way, I guess he bid him. And then, when they come on up the house, and of course...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That's, what kind of water is that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That's what they call a carnival. Oh, it's lovely. Carnival glass, I think. And then I got two or three like this with size handles on them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those are shower gifts?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. Oh, really nice. And then the old stacks of glasses, you know, and fruit bowls and little saustiches and, you know, that kind of stuff. I wonder why they call that carnival glass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what they used to give the prizes when they loved carnivals?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, it looks too nice for that. Maybe it's the colors because it has the... it reflects so many different colors in it. It has lots of different colors in it. Um... Carrie Reynolds has one almost exactly like this. Only it's primary doesn't look like it's ruffled in it so much. It looks like the same color and everything. Well, that's carnival glass.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Now, what's depression glass?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You see that at advertising. Well... These guys are telling me it's depression glass. Well, you were telling me a story about the dog bit... Oh, yeah, I bit that guy. But anyway, they come up on the house while all the rockets woke me up, see. I was just a little kid. And so I went and looked out the window. I think it was summertime and I think they had the window raised up so far. And I went and took my head out. And as I took my head out, I seen a ball of fire right down below me. And so some guy just up and pulled the shotgun off, you know, in the air. And he didn't have it straight up. So the next day I went out the house right up just above the window where the aliens are on the roof, you know. Birds out all around the place. So the whole house fell down now. It was still there. That old shot didn't miss my aim. I got very far. He just stopped up at the house and he just banged, you know, just like that. And I had my head stuck out to it and I looked down there. Boy, I could...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when did you first start working in the mill? That was before you two were married, right?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah. I started working down in the mill during World War I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That early?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: About 1918. That was... only the United States got into it. And, well, I don't know, all the kids was able to work, could get a job, because they pulled so many men out, you know, it was a war. They put boys and women to work. And that's when I started working, picking engines down in the mill. I think I was about 17, 18.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gee, if that now, if that, because weren't you born in 1903?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. You must have been even younger. Let me see. If it was 18 and you was born in three, you would have been 15. I think we got in in 17. Seventeenth. So you must have been about 14, 15. No, I wasn't that young. There was something wrong with my story there. It must have been right after the World War. It seemed like I was 17, 18 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they still have a 10 -hour day when you first started working there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, eight hours. No, I think about 1914 it lasted a 10 -hour day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the working conditions like when you went into the mill?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I guess the Wobblies had already cleaned it up as far as the 10 -hour day goes. Yeah, yeah, the Wobblies had already done their good work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the work like? Did you get many breaks during the day or anything like that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, the work wasn't too hard. They always had a lot of men. That's before they tried to double the work up on guys like they do now. No, they just, kids go down there and pick an engine, set the guys up, pick the engines off as they go by off the boards. And it wasn't no hard work. The pay wasn't so hot, $3 .40 a day. That was a lot of money for a kid that didn't have any money though.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, it wasn't six days a week.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, when they first started the mill up down here, they started up two shifts, a 12 -hour day. Yeah, 12 -hour days. Kept the old mill going day and night. But I don't know, that run, that started up about 1906, I think, when they got the mill started right in there someplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think it was about 1914, didn't they?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they finally got down to 10 hours and then finally down to 8. I think about 1914 it lasted a 10 -hour day. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, when I've been talking to people in Potlatch, I've heard quite a bit about all the foreigners that worked there. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know or see much of the Greeks and Italians and Japanese in there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. I seen that mill, but, well, that was during the, let me see, that'd be World War I. World War II was, there wasn't over six men in that sawmill then that was white. It was all Greeks, Italians, Japs. And they had Japs, Italians, Greeks.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they had four carriages down there, you know, and they had two men on a carriage. Long time, I was the only one that was, wasn't a Wapper or a Greek. All the rest of them.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But then during the Depression is when they got thinned out a lot. A lot of them took off during the Depression and never come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, were they, did they stick to themselves pretty much? The Greeks or the Italians?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they were, oh, thank God they used to be in the mill in the supper time, you know. They'd all get around a bunch, them Greeks. And there was Alec McDonald and I on another, there was only about three or four guys in there outside the Sawyers, that wasn't Greek. And the guys, they'd get to yakking like a bunch of chickens over there. And Alec McDonald would say, hey, you sons of beasts. I'd just get talking, go back to Greece. Oh, they used to give them a hard time. Oh, yeah, they'd congregate by themselves. They didn't associate. Well, I guess you couldn't blow them.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But anyway, well, there's only about one Greek left in the mountains, I guess I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gus Demas?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, old Gus. I guess he was the only one left who, the Italians down there. That's true. Well, I guess old Gus was the last one in the bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that foreigners were sort of discriminated against so far as getting the better jobs? Do you think they had a harder time than the Americans?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I think they were favored.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. Well, there was a reason for it. I don't know if, I can't prove when I say that, but they'd do anything for a job. And an ordinary guy around here, none of them ever married, you know. They was all single bucks and they'd all bat together and it didn't cost them much to live. But they'd kick back to their boss, see. That's what I've heard now. I've never seen that, but I think there must be something to it. Because they'd kick back so much a month to their boss, and the boss would work them up in the job.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: So they didn't have much trouble working up and getting the better jobs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder how come the Greeks were in the sawmill and the Italians were in the yards? I mean, why they worked out that way? Did you ever think about, figure out why that was?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I guess they weren't very, very hard Italians in the sawmill, but there was a lot of them out in the planer and they worked on this. That's when they had little railroads run all over the yard out there, they'd haul lumber around on. They had a lot of them working out there on the sections and building tracks. And then there was some, quite a few of them worked on the green chain.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But, I don't know why that was. The Greeks and Italians didn't mix very good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I guess they don't to this day, do they?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They're the kind of... But that doesn't mean much. That doesn't mean you... I don't really know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any friends with any of the Greeks in the sawmill?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, yeah. After World War II, there was old Gus and John Gano.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: John Gano was a good friend of his. Yeah, him and I were good friends. And Gus, I knew him pretty good, but I didn't know him because I did John. But then there was John's brother, Tony. And, yeah, they were all good guys. Yeah, they were. Got along with them all right. But the rest of them, they just disappeared. I don't know where they went to.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: One of them, I don't know. He, like I say, he squeezed his money. And when the depression come, I think his name was... I don't know what his name was. They called him Sleepy Jim. So, whatever it was, but Sleepy Jim. And he was kind of a laggard guy. He walked with a, you know, like he was all disjointed. Well, he saved his money. So, I guess when depression come, I pulled out. Some of the other Greeks told me, I guess, him and another guy went together and they bought a big hotel down in Seattle. And then they made money on that, my guys, and then bought another one. And I guess they got to be millionaires, both of them two Greeks. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well. Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Japanese? They were pretty much separate from everybody else too, weren't they? Who?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. Oh, yeah. They had their own boarding house. Company built a boarding house just for the Jap down there. Potlatch. Yeah. They kept themselves pretty well. Well, there's one little Jap used to janitor in the store and down at the main office. Wapenabi, I think his name is something like that. Yeah. Just a little fella. And he had a little wife and two or three little kids. But when Pearl Harbor hit, I guess they just railroad them out to a concentration camp, I guess.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. I heard that there was talk that he was an agent of some kind. And maybe that's what they thought about all the Japanese then. I don't know. Well, anyway, this guy, he was just a janitor. But yet, he had a really expensive camera. And he'd talk to different guys to take him around the country, you know. They'd go to Lewiston and he'd take him pictures. And nobody thought nothing about it, you know. Till after it was over with. And they wondered how the heck he could afford such a camera. And that was really some camera. And of course, Pearl Harbor, that's why they figured he was just working for a spy. And suddenly taking pictures and sending them all the mail out here from Lewiston, I suppose this one, and bams or whatever they were. They'd think he'd get around. He'd pay guys for taking him. And you know, a man just working for that kind of job them days didn't make that kind of money. So anyway, everybody just goes along with it until Pearl Harbor broke loose and then all of a sudden they was ready to take him out and just get him alive. He was a good little guy up until that point. But I got along and he disappeared out. I was reading the other day about a lot of them were good citizens. Took them and just hauled them off like cattle in trucks, you know, and shoved them in them concentration camps. And it wasn't fair.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Didn't a lot of them know about Pearl Harbor before it happened and get out of here?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, yeah. They started before Pearl Harbor. They started to disappear. A lot of them started to disappear out of the potlice. I don't know where they went. Oh, there was one little Jap Edgeman down there. I worked with him. And oh, way back in the early 20s. Way back in the early 20s. He kept on saying, someday Jap Engle will win this country. I'd say, oh, they couldn't lick one side of it. Oh, yes they will. Someday Jap Engle will win this country. I guess you know, I believe they was planning that a long time before Pearl Harbor. He'd say, someday Jap Engle will win this country. Maybe he could just see that it was coming because of the history of the thing. I mean, they got a different way of looking at history over there in the east, in the Orient, than we do here. Well, I think the country must have been planned. You know, just waiting until they got ready to go. Probably that might have been 10, 15 years ahead of Pearl Harbor. See, I would have thought that those foreign groups now, the idea I've got from Gus Demas, I talked to him some, is that these Greeks and Italians didn't speak much English. And neither did the Japanese. I think that would be pretty hard on groups like that to be a part of the community if they couldn't speak an English language. Well, most of them tried to speak a little bit. Old John Gaynor and Tony, they, you had to be used to them to understand them. But they got so they could talk pretty good. But anyway, they, no they weren't bad guys, but both of them wasn't even citizens. I don't know, Gus would tell me one time he went back to Greece, oh, six years ago, seven years ago, maybe more than that time goes. But anyway, he took an extra pair of shoes with him, you know, and his cousin was over there. He used to be at the mill, but he went back to Greece and got married. So Gus was over there. And my Gus, the people support, you know, they just almost fought over that extra pair of shoes, old Gus had. So finally he gives a pair of shoes to his cousin, and Gus didn't pay long. I guess he gave a lot of money to his mother. He was pretty well healed one time. Saved his money all his life. Well, I guess he gave a lot of his money away over there. He kept enough to live the rest of his life over here. He gave a lot of his clothes away, he took clothes over there. People just so hard up over there, you just couldn't say no to them. He mentioned to me that his sister wasn't in very good shape over there. I wouldn't be surprised if he didn't help her out quite a bit. No, I imagine not. Yeah. But he didn't like it over there. He went over there to stay, I guess. After he was there, he bought him a ticket back to the USA. Yeah. Well, when those guys, the Greeks, I heard the Greeks did quite a bit of gambling and the Italians did quite a bit of bootlegging in here. Bootlegging wine, anyway. Well, the Greeks weren't as bad as the Degas. Used to be an old Deagol Mike, they called him, anyway. Mike Petrogalla was his name. He was a bootlegger from way back. You probably heard him say, when you buy a Model D Ford, you can have any color you want. I thought it was black. Well, that was the truth. That was the only color. But old Mike, he'd get a new Ford. But before he'd take it on the road, he'd take it in, paint it yellow. Paint it yellow before he'd ever take it out on the road. He'd always tell old Deagol Mike's car when he got down the road because it was the real yellow. I didn't know why, but he'd take it and paint it yellow before it ever went on the road. Do you think that there was much... I've heard about that tricks and jokes were a big thing in the mill. It was a way past the time, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember there being very much of that when you were in the mill, jobbing other guys and playing practical jokes?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: One guy told me that he remembers a favorite one was sticking grease in a guy's glove. I don't know about that. The only thing that was kind of fun, one night down there, they... I discovered the fire, but I was riding the number one carriage. You look up on the carriage, you can look out the window, and they had a conveyor run out there. It runs out to the fire hole. Carriage sawdust out of the fire hole. I looked down there and I seen a fire burning. So either they yell at me and I jumped off the carriage and I had a fire hose up there. And so it had to run like this at the edge of the mill here. The fire hose was here. When they turned it on, it was rolled up on the ridge here. But you had to run it down here and then poke it out the window down on the fire. So that was going to be the engineer who turned the water on. I don't know George. George Turner. He was one of them. But they went down there, you know, they'd run that thing down and pull it out and then haul it and turn it around. Well, to turn it around easy, I just gave it a whin. Of course, that old hose just straightened out. Them guys went backwards over the tracks there on the carriage. I'm going to drown. And they always were. I done that on purpose. Well, they haul it and turn it around, you know. But it would have been a whole other straight. But they had that thing that fell out the window. Of course, that old pressure hit that hose. It just straightened out and stuck them guys with them.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: They always blamed me for doing that on purpose. You didn't do that on purpose anyway. I said, I'll turn it around, so I turned it around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would a fire like that get going?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't know how to try that fire. Could be friction. Like a bearing got hot on that chain or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the fire hole? You said they got a fire hole there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, a fire hole. That's where they got the fires to heat make the steam. Boiler rooms. Down there where they watch the fires and boilers up above. Down there where they have to keep sawdust from the fires down there on all them boilers. I don't know why they call it the fire hole. They always called it. But, you know, them guys, I didn't do it on purpose. They run the hose down there and hope that it went and turn it around. So turn it around I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you going down there from Princeton every day to work in the mill in those days when you first worked there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the early years you worked there?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, first tired out, another kid and I, we stayed at the boarding house down at Potlatch. Then we come home on weekends. And we both had bicycles. And we got tired of that. The boarding house was grubbed and I had to play that on you after a while. So, no, a long time I lived up here on the old place and working night shift. And I'd ride my bike to the mill and back every day. Nights, no headlights on it. I think that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You motorcycled? Huh?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: A motorcycle, not a bicycle. No, a bicycle. I didn't get the motorcycle until I was 23 years old I guess. Before I got a motorbike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what? Was there a real difference, a difference between the local kids who came from the farming country around here, let's say, or the backwoods country and the people who were town people that lived in Potlatch?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, there was a lot of friction. Town people, oh, you know, the farm kids, you know, the backwoods. Yeah, there was a lot of friction between town people. Yes, it was awful hard down in Potlatch going to high school. You know, the town kids just about run that country kid out. Did one have a friend that they used to, she had to ride a horse to school way out in the country, you know, in order to come to school. And they'd do like that, hold their nose, cause they said she smelled like a horse. She took it until she couldn't take it and then she quit. But I had a better deal than they did most, because I had to work in Potlatch for my board and then I would go to school. And so after a while I got considered a town kid. Huh.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Why did you live with the better people?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, I don't know if they were better people, but they sure thought they were. They thought they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this is when you were going to high school, you were working for your board at the same time? You were working at the house you lived at?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, you know, you worked with the people you know. They'd take it, and the thing you'd do then was to have a little servant girl. And you'd go in and get a family to live with, and they'd feed you for doing the ironing and the dishwashing and taking care of the kids and stuff like that in the evenings and mornings so they could go to their parties and whatnots. I worked one year and a half for the store manager down there, and then I worked another year for... That's as far as he made it. Him and I was down in the bottom basement of that old pool hall. It was his idea, he was really the inventor of that old ice cream, you know, it was on a stick you know, ice cream on a stick and then chocolate around the ideas. But he didn't know exactly the formula, he had the experiment so I helped him out of it. And of course, every stick wouldn't work where I could eat it. I put in a pile of hot ice cream. The way they do it, you know. That sounds like a pretty good job. Well, it wasn't no good for nothing else. So, I don't know, we worked down there putting her in a biggie every night, trying to get the temperature just right so you get that ice cream just cold you know, and you dip it in this hot chocolate and pull it out. And that would leave her coating the chocolate on. But if you didn't have the chocolate hot enough, the ice cream wasn't cold enough, you know, it just wouldn't work. So you had your thermometer there, you'd get that chocolate just boiling hot you know, and you'd get that ice cream on a stick and shove it down that chocolate and pull it up. You get everything just right, you could just take them and just come in there and make them just, you know, listen to it you know. But I don't know how many chocolates he'd put down, that didn't work. He didn't have a chocolate hot enough or the ice cream wasn't cold enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he just sell them at his place or did he sell them around the country, other places?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When he got them, he tried to sell them right down his own joint down there. Well then he shipped them out to other towns. Yeah, he got the patent on them I think and then they went out. But I don't know, when I went to Portland, I went in the joint down there in Portland you know, and asked for an ice cream, uh, X -Flow pie. It looked at me like I had horns on, it didn't know what I was talking about. But, anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anna, was that, was that what an, did a number of kids do that, come in to live in Potlatch to go to high school? Or were you special when you went on a few that did that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, no, anybody that could come that could stand it, you know, and make any difference how they got there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When, the year I graduated was, there was only 14 or 15?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Mm -hmm. 15 kids I guess graduated that year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 15 kids from Potlatch and?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And all the country around it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean, did the kids from Harvard, did they, if you wanted to go to high school in Potlatch, let's say, would you pretty much have to live in the town if you came from so far away as Harvard?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Some kids came in every day that far?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: One thing about Potlatch, they run the school, they run everything. They hired the teachers of Potlatch Limber Company, they hired the teachers and they built the schoolhouse, they kept the schoolhouse up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they let you go without paying?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. And the district we come from had to pay something for us.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Oh, did, for high school?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But probably the family didn't pay. No, no, the family didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did your family just, did your family think it would be easier for you to go to school by living in Potlatch? Was that the idea?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, yes, they did because we had no way of getting there. And we had to go on to school, and so that was what we were doing. Well, I had been working in Potlatch anyway in the summertime, you know, and so I wasn't new at that job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then when I went to high school, why?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: That's about the only way a country kid could go to school, you know, they wouldn't have had to go to town and live, you know. If you lived two or three miles, you know, you could ride a horse. But like living up at Harwood, I'd have to go down there and stay. Yeah, that's... When they lived over here, moved to the family, finally moved over to Princeton, then she'd walk the school back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, and your family moved to Princeton? You just walked to Potlatch?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, my family moved down to Princeton, and so we started walking. Most of the time we walked the track, railroad track.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard on you when you were a kid to be working in well -to -do people's houses and to go to school? I mean, did you feel that was a hardship then?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yes, it was harder working for my board lot than it was walking. Because, well, you had so much work to do, you know, for the family in order to earn your keep. Well, they'd treat kids, you know, like that. Not all families, but some of them. Just like the white men used to do the Negroes, you know. You were just a servant. That's what you was. You were just a servant. How was it that aunt of mine done you when she was... She'd made a dentist out of Potlatch. For a pay, she'd give you some old dress or something. Yeah, she said that if I'd come and work for her, well, she'd give me a dollar a week, you know, besides my board. So, I changed. I was staying in a good place, but a person needs money too. So, I went to work for her, and I don't think I ever collected the dollar. She would give me some old block she'd worn out, you know, and she'd say, well, you take this for your pay, and we'll see. It's about worth six dollars. And so that'd be six weeks I'd work for that thing. Probably I would never have it on. But, you know, that's the way it was. Yeah. And I was glad to walk. Kids, I think kids can be used pretty easily that way, you know.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Did they have electric irons that time?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I suppose, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe, I don't know, was it electric or did she just heat it up on a stove or something?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: It must have been electric. But she had two little kids, and she would dress them in the morning, and then at noon she would change their clothes, and they were ready for, tell their daddy to come home. So that was two sets of clothes every day, besides his white shirts he ran down, wore down to the store. I was so worried time I'd get ready for bed, and I got up in the morning, got the kids up and gave them their breakfast. For almost a day of boarding, I bet you didn't eat enough to take a hang of it. Well, not there I didn't. Oh boy, no, that was cruel days. But at Elsie's I got more than I wanted, more than enough. Well, that's what I say, it just depends on who you work for. Do you think these people who were living on Nob Hill, I've heard about them, that quite a few of them lived above their means, really. Oh boy, well kind of a funny thing, during the war, World War II, there was a family living up there on Nob Hill, and he was with a foreman down at the mill. And they were living high. But anyway, down at the garage at Potlatch, the man by the name was Tableton, he was running the garage. But all of a sudden the government pulled out, I guess, that anybody didn't pay that much bill, cash bill, you know. But for that month, well, you couldn't get no more credit. Well, I didn't know nothing about it, but it just happened I made a little money and I went in and paid my bill up there. And so that law went into effect, and so I know I came up to the garage to get some gas, because I had to drive, and I lived on the other place up here, driving back and forth to work. And this high -gluten lady, she drove in there and told her to fill her tank. And so, I guess she drove in, and so they wanted her money, she said charge it. And, well, he went back and looked on you, they had pages and pages of names there, you know, in Els. If your name was on that list, well, you didn't get no gas. So anyway, I'm looking over, she says, you'll have to pay for it, but she didn't have the money to pay for it. So they drained the gas out of her tank.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And, of course, I, you know, they just drained the gas back out, that's all they could do. The law said they had to have the money. And so I pulled right behind her, and I finally got her shoved out of there. I pulled up and told her to fill her up. The guy looked at me and went back in, and I went in with him. Well, I guess I shouldn't have been, but he was going down the list of names, you know. And I didn't have my name on it, because like I said, I just got the hitter lucky, and he wasn't paying my bill up. And so I guess my name wasn't on it. And I acted like it almost hurt his feelings. That kind of a thing. He was so sure, you know, he was going to tell me to go, you know. Just got out of there. But I guess my name wasn't on the list, and I believe it really hurt his feelings, but I got you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, those people, those people that you worked for that weren't, that didn't care how much you had to eat really, were they just, they just used you like a servant, pretty much?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, they just, no matter, you know, you had it to do. But the first people I stayed with, I was invited to the table to eat, you know, with the family. But the second time that I wasn't, I had to eat in the kitchen. I had to serve then, and then go back in the kitchen and eat what I could. But, and, I don't know, I don't, now that I'm old, I don't care much for them.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You know what?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't care too much for them. But, she was pretty good to brag on me. Well, my girl does this, and my girl does that. My girl. But I got in trouble more times there because...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Who was that, huh?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Um, Walters. Will Walters, yeah. He, um, ran the store down there, and he would bring, you know, candy and stuff home. But, you know, I knew better than to touch it. But I had company, my girlfriend from up at Harvard stopped in to see me. And, um, she saw candy there, and when she saw candy, she just hated it, you know. And she'd eat and eat and eat, and I didn't know how to stop her, I was too dumb to say, well, that... That's not for me. And holy cow, next morning did I get it. They just don't sound like nice people as far as... No, they wouldn't. It seems like they shouldn't have understood that you were as good as they were, even if you were working for them. Yeah. Well, there was a whole lot like that, too, that turned out to be the old manager at Laird, that I put up a road down here. He was so important that us Hicks was supposed to get off the road for him, until I taught him a lesson. Then he found out my girlfriend wasn't paying off. But then that was the idea, it was just, you know, he sat there, you couldn't say he fired a chauffeur from when he got in trouble, but yet you couldn't say he was riding down the street and everybody gets thrown off the road so he goes through, and he wasn't enjoying that. He was enjoying that, watching all the Hicks get off the road for him. But it was nice to be bragged on, you know. It was nice to have the ladies brag on you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She bragged on you to other ladies? To her friends?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah. You know, well, my girl does this and that, well, I told my girl to do this and that, you know. But, uh... Well, they had a two -class society. Now they'd have dancers down in that old gymnasium. And just to dance for the big shots. A little eye or a herd, but we couldn't go there, you know, we were outcasts. And, of course, we'd go in and get up on the balcony and look down and watch the High Buckle Bucks dance down there, you know. Anyway, I don't know, some guy got a whole bunch of big bombs someplace. I was up there and I didn't know it. But I guess you throw them down the dance floor, you know. It didn't take long to break up the dance. And over there, he was so furious. Oh, if you could have found out who'd done that. Found out. You never found out. But that was the last dance they had, was that High Buckle Bucks dance. About two classes. That was the last time they ever had a dance. That broke it up. No more of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the houses on Nob Hill have servants? Have a girl working for them? Was that the common way to do it? Or only the most well -to -do?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Anybody they could get, it was to work for them for nothing, you know. And if they could keep you, well, that was the big thing.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: But, uh... Mrs. Thompson was a jewel. She was awful tight. She'd be at the table and she'd look all over. Do you want a slice of...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: No, she said, what do you want?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: If I said bread or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whole slice or half a slice?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Of course, I'd always say a half a slice. But that, if I'd have said a whole slice, she would have... She was generous and all that, but she wasn't one to waste anything. As soon as I understood that, I just... And she was very careful to see that I got enough sleep. And, um, she was the one that taught me the most about how to get along with those kind of people. She was one of them herself. Yes, she was one. And she was...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: A gracious lady or whatever you call it. She truly was.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And then, her kids were bigger than general babies too. And so they didn't need any babysitting with them. I just...

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: The kids would ask, can I do this and that or the other thing?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I'd have to decide whether they could or they couldn't if their mother had... If they hadn't asked her ahead of time. But that was a real nice place to study. She taught you how to get along. Was that like...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there really certain things you were supposed to do or not supposed to do when you were dealing with those people? I mean certain ways of acting?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And she told me, well, for instance... One thing, it doesn't mean anything, but it's one thing I remember especially. She... I always wash my hands before I go to do anything. Well, this time I had the soap in my hand and I was just really washing. And she says, well, dear, I would just take a little soap and then wash my hands with water. I just wouldn't wash the soap. Well, that's really what I was doing. And so that was one way she taught me. And just little suggestions in that way with anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were living with those store people, were you supposed to not speak to them until you were spoken to and that kind of thing? Or did they have rules like that?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I don't really know how people are supposed to be when they're supposed to be waiting on other people.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: You spoke to them kind of around the corner that you were spoken to, wasn't you?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Yeah, and you don't interrupt any conversation or... Kind of an irony thing happened, you know. Like I say, my aunt, she made a dentist. And of course I had to work for her. And well, my guess, you know, she was just lowbrow, you know, just nobody, you know, just a servant. Well, it turned out I started going there and married her, see. Well, I had not only worked for her, but she knew a lot of the going on around the house, you know. I got my ass killed. A woman would scream to the top of her voice. She'd scream and scream and scream. And she was... But then long, long afterward I saw her and Potlatch had advanced on there and she had gone down to change her shoes and we had to do a more comfortable pair of shoes.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And she said, how are you, Anna?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She said, I'm extremely glad you got into the family. She was a big guy.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I'm pretty happy about it too, except for you, lady. Yes. But she knew. Yes. Yes, that got her going. I got married by a guy, you know. She knew Anna knew going along around that house with her. She wanted to get up in the morning, you know. She'd leave me a note here, leave me a note there, and if I'd miss a note, holy... Mmm, things would erupt. One time she left a note on the kitchen stove. I guess it was kettle sitting on it or something. Anyway, I looked around and I didn't see the note. And what she wanted me to do was put a boiler on the stove and fill it full of water so the laundry maid could come in later and do the washing. And I didn't see that and I didn't put the water on the stove, of course. And I just did what I had to do otherwise. And pretty soon the laundry woman come and... No water on the stove. No hot water to start with. Oh man, Pearl was mad. Because she was wanting to get that woman out of there, you know, before noon. But if she left her notes in the same place so I could find them. Sometimes you could go in the living room and if she happened to be there the night before when she thought of what she wanted me to do, she'd write the note and leave it there. Just inconsiderate. Well, she wasn't at all. You know, she wasn't a rich girl herself. She was just raised on a farm down here, you know. You know, families had a habit of doing that. Maybe they'd have a half a dozen kids or more. But they'd pick one kid out and they'd lavish everything. Well, it happened to be that family. It was the older kids. They had to work and do without and everything else. But the last two of them... I worked with my granddad down there in the Hayen, you know. And my uncle, he was what, three years older than I was. But his mother wouldn't let him work on that. But when I turned to work in Hayfield, it wasn't good enough work for him. I could go out there and work all night. But he couldn't, no. The most he could do was take the cars around town, get closer, stuff the hall out. And my aunt, his sister, she just sat around and looked pretty. My mother would go down and have my mother in the kitchen getting dinner ready for the men, you know, cooking. And my daughter, she sat out in the front porch bag, I said, look pretty. She couldn't get her hands filed or nothing. Well, that's the way they raised her, see. She is the greatest, lovely daughter there ever was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she got herself a dentist, right?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: She went into... Yes, he was. And that shot her into the upper crust. And she never let anybody forget it. But then they... One more day, they did a divorce. She was suing for divorce.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: When he died?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: And he died. And then she married somebody, but I forgot who else. You've been around probably a lot more than I have, but you know, I like how I worked on construction in Portland. You know, real millionaire, real rich man. Harry's wife, don't make a difference. You know, you'd never know it. I didn't know how to work down there. But I was working on a man named Bowles. He made his money in World War I in the trip yard down there on the coast. Bowles. He built a big, well, it was a $300 ,000 house up on the console crest there in Portland. And I worked on that job there. Well, he'd come around, you know, with his old clasps he had on. I thought he was just a sidewalk bum, you know. He'd stop you and I'd ask you about something and talk to you. Maybe talk to you. I wondered why I was talking to him when the boss didn't come around. You know, he'd keep holding you up and talking to you.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: While you are supposed to be working.

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: I couldn't figure that out. The boss didn't come around and tell me to get to work. Later I found out I was talking to this guy who was building a house, you know. And his wife says the only thing is they'd never drive the same car. He had one of them old Franklin cars. Air -cooled fragments. And he had one that wasn't a new one. It was an old one. His daughter, when she'd come around, she'd drive the fastest car. But the old lady had an old car. She'd drive her out and she was friendly enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's the difference between the old rich and the new rich? Or the ones that are trying to get rich, maybe?

speaker: Emmett or Anna Utt
words: Well, they're just two -bit millionaires. There's the worst kind of millionaires there is. I'm two -bit, but, you know, somebody gets a few dollars more than the other guy. And everybody's supposed to slit them, flit them when they go down the road or something. Now I always call them two -bit millionaires.
